title: Installing a first-flush diverter on a water butt
subtitle: Diverting the first 10 to 20 litres of rainwater off the roof to improve water quality in the storage tank
excerpt: The first flush of rain off a roof carries the highest concentration of bird droppings, atmospheric particulates, and degraded roofing material. A first-flush diverter shunts that contaminated water away from the storage tank and connects to the downpipe only after the initial flush has passed. The result is cleaner water in the butt, less algae growth, and longer filter life if the water is used for irrigation.
sourceNotes: CIRIA SuDS guidance on rainwater quality. WRAP guidance on water butt installation. DEFRA rainwater harvesting quality standards (non-potable use).

glossaryTerms.definition: Collecting and storing rainfall from roof surfaces for non-potable uses such as garden irrigation, toilet flushing, or laundry.

content.text: Standard water butt diverters connect to the downpipe and fill the butt as rain falls. The first few litres off the roof carry the highest pollutant load: pollen, bird droppings, gutter debris, and oxidised roofing felt. Filling the butt from clean rainfall produces water that is clearer, lower in coliform bacteria, and less likely to promote algae growth. A first-flush diverter costs 20 to 30 pounds and replaces the standard diverter. It works by filling a standpipe with the first flush before routing subsequent runoff into the butt.

The diverter body replaces the standard downpipe diverter. It has a vertical standpipe branch below the connection point, sized to hold 10 to 20 litres. When rain starts, the first water fills this standpipe. Once the standpipe is full, subsequent water overflows from the top into the horizontal pipe that feeds the butt. The standpipe has a slow-release drain at the base (a ball valve or a drilled orifice) so it empties between rain events, ready to catch the next first flush. On a 40 m² roof, 1 mm of rainfall produces 40 litres of runoff. A 15-litre standpipe captures the first flush from a typical 0.4 mm shower event.
First-flush diverter unit (sized for the downpipe diameter: 68 mm or 110 mm are the two UK standard sizes)
Standpipe (supplied with the diverter, or a short section of soil pipe at the same diameter)
PTFE tape and a pipe sealant for the threaded connections
Remove the existing standard downpipe diverter. Cut the downpipe at the diverter outlet with a pipe cutter or hacksaw.

Fit the first-flush diverter body into the cut section. The diverter body has an inlet at the top (connecting to the downpipe above), an outlet side-arm that connects to the butt, and a standpipe connection pointing downward.

Connect the standpipe to the downward port. The standpipe should hang vertically and hold 10 to 20 litres. For a 68 mm pipe, 1 metre holds about 3.6 litres. For a 110 mm pipe, 1 metre holds 9.5 litres. Adjust the length for the volume you want to capture.
Fit the slow-release drain at the base of the standpipe. A 2 to 3 mm drilled orifice allows the standpipe to empty over 12 to 24 hours, ready for the next rain event.
Connect the side arm to the
butt with a diverter pipe or flexible hose.

attrs.symptom: Standpipe not emptying between rain events

content.text: The drain orifice is blocked with grit or algae. Remove and clean the orifice insert. Consider fitting a small stainless steel mesh filter above the orifice.
The standpipe volume is too large for the catchment area: light rain events fill the standpipe but not enough flow reaches the overflow to fill the butt. Reduce standpipe length by half.
